evals.prompt: We have a function that takes an age (integer 0-150). How should we test it?
evals.expected_output: Should check qa-context.md or ask. Should apply equivalence partitioning + boundary value analysis: partitions are negative, 0-150, above max, non-integer, null; boundaries are -1, 0, 1, 149, 150, 151. Should produce a concrete table of cases. Should suggest representative test names and recommend running these in pytest / jest / appropriate framework. Should remind that the goal is one representative per partition plus the boundary cases — not 150 cases for 'all valid ages.'
assertions: Reads qa-context.md or asks for it
Avoids testing every age in the valid range

evals.prompt: Our checkout has 4 boolean flags (member, has_coupon, international, expedited) and 3 price tiers. That's 48 combinations. Do we test all?
evals.expected_output: Should not insist on full Cartesian — 48 is borderline manageable but pairwise covers the critical interactions in ~6-10 cases. Should walk through using pairwise (PICT or similar) to generate the case set covering every pair of input values. Should mention constraints — e.g., 'expedited' may not be valid with one of the price tiers — and how to express them in PICT. Should recommend pairwise plus explicit hand-picked edge cases (e.g., the highest-revenue combination). Cross-references the test-strategy skill for risk-weighting.
assertions: Identifies pairwise as the right technique

evals.prompt: how do i test our order state machine
evals.expected_output: Should treat the casual phrasing as a valid trigger. Should recommend state-transition testing: enumerate states (Cart, PendingPayment, Paid, Shipped, Delivered, Cancelled), enumerate events / transitions, produce a test per valid transition plus tests for invalid transitions (Delivered → ship should reject). Should provide a concrete transition table with test labels. Should mention that the state diagram lives somewhere — keep it in sync with code. Should NOT recommend testing only the happy path.
assertions: Recommends keeping the state model in sync with code
Does not stop at happy path only

evals.prompt: We want to test our JSON parser. It needs to handle arbitrary inputs. Suggestions?
evals.expected_output: Should recommend property-based testing as the primary technique — parsers are exactly where property tests shine. Suggest hypothesis (Python), fast-check (JS), jqwik (JVM), FsCheck (.NET), or fuzz testing if on Go. Properties to assert: (a) parse(toString(x)) == x for valid x (round-trip); (b) parse(garbage) raises a specific exception type, never panics; (c) parser respects max-depth / max-size limits. Should also add example-based tests for known edge cases: empty, very deep nesting, Unicode strings, numeric edge cases (Infinity, NaN, very large). Should warn that property tests with poor properties are no better than no tests. Cross-reference go-test (fuzzing) and pytest.
assertions: Suggests concrete properties (round-trip, error shape, limits)

evals.prompt: We have a Scenario Outline with 80 rows of test data in our Cucumber feature. The team complains it's slow and unreadable.
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose the anti-pattern: an 80-row Scenario Outline is usually a unit-level test in disguise. Recommend extracting the underlying logic and writing parameterized unit tests in the host language (parametrize in pytest, @Theory in JUnit 5, table-driven in Go) with the same 80 cases. The Cucumber layer should keep only the user-facing behavior — maybe 3-5 representative scenarios that exercise the integration of components. Should cross-reference bdd-anti-patterns. Should remind that 80 rows × end-to-end overhead = wasted minutes per CI run.
assertions: Identifies Scenario Outline of 80 rows as anti-pattern
Recommends moving to parameterized unit tests in host runner
Keeps a small representative subset at BDD layer

evals.prompt: What edge cases am I likely missing in my tests for a date-handling function?
evals.expected_output: Should give an error-guessing checklist for dates: (1) DST transitions (spring-forward and fall-back); (2) leap years (Feb 29); (3) year boundaries (Dec 31 / Jan 1); (4) timezone offsets (UTC, half-hour zones like India, Nepal, Iran); (5) Unix epoch (1970-01-01) and just-before; (6) far-future dates (year 9999, year 10000); (7) parsing locale-specific date strings; (8) null / empty inputs; (9) ISO 8601 vs locale-specific formats; (10) midnight precisely. Should mention that property-based testing can also flush out date bugs (cross-reference). Should remind the user to write a regression test for any production date bug they've encountered.
assertions: Lists at least 6 concrete date edge cases
Includes DST, leap year, timezone offsets
Mentions property-based testing as a complement
Recommends regression tests for past production bugs
